revision.text:
'''厨子'''（ずし）とは、[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置する[[仏具]]の一種。広義の意味では[[仏壇]]も厨子に含まれる。 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。
[[玉虫厨子]]が有名。

'''厨子'''（ずし）とは、[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置する[[仏具]]の一種。広義の意味では[[仏壇]]も厨子に含まれる。 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。
[[玉虫厨子]]が有名。
[[Category:仏具|ずし]]

[[玉虫厨子]]が有名。

{{Buddhism}} '''厨子'''（ずし）とは、[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置する[[仏具]]の一種。広義の意味では[[仏壇]]も厨子に含まれる。 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場しており、主に葬儀式においては学会が祭壇に据える。
[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の厨子が有名。近年、小型の仏壇として見直されている。

[[Category:仏具|すし]]

{{Buddhism}} '''厨子'''（ずし）とは、[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置する[[仏具]]の一種。広義の意味では[[仏壇]]も厨子に含まれる。 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場しており、主に葬儀式においては創価学会が祭壇に据える。[[日蓮正宗]]の葬儀でも祭壇に置かれ、導師本尊が掲げられる。
[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の厨子が有名。近年、小型の仏壇として見直されている。

[[ファイル:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|玉虫厨子]] {{Buddhism}} '''厨子'''（ずし）は、[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置する[[仏具]]の一種である。広義では[[仏壇]]も厨子に含まれる。
== 概要 == 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場しており、主に葬儀式においては[[創価学会]]が祭壇に据える。[[日蓮正宗]]の葬儀でも祭壇に置かれ、導師本尊が掲げられる。
近年、小型の仏壇として見直されている。
歴史的な作品としては[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の厨子が有名。
<gallery> ファイル:Miroku Zushi.jpg|木造弥勒菩薩半跏像を収める厨子（[[興福寺]]） </gallery>
== 関連項目 == * [[玉虫厨子]] * [[庄内厨子]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:すし}} [[Category:仏具]]
{{Buddhism-stub}}

[[ファイル:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|[[玉虫厨子]]（[[法隆寺]]・[[国宝]]）]] '''厨子'''（ずし）は、[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置する[[仏具]]の一種である。広義では[[仏壇]]も厨子に含まれる，'''龕'''とも呼ばれます。。
== 概要 == 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場しており、主に葬儀式においては[[創価学会]]が祭壇に据える。[[日蓮正宗]]の葬儀でも祭壇に置かれ、導師本尊が掲げられる。
近年、小型の仏壇として見直されている。
歴史的な作品としては[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の赤漆文欟木御厨子が有名。
<gallery> ファイル:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|赤漆文欟木御厨子（[[正倉院]]） ファイル:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|竹厨子（[[法隆寺献納宝物]]・国宝） ファイル:Miroku Zushi.jpg|木造弥勒菩薩半跏像を収める厨子（[[興福寺]]・[[重要文化財]]<ref>「厨子入木造弥勒菩薩半跏像」として</ref>） </gallery>

また[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置する[[仏具]]の一種である。広義では[[仏壇]]も厨子に含まれる。'''龕'''（がん）とも呼ばれる。
== 概要 == 起源は中国で主に台所用品を納める容器のことを指した、後に日本へと輸入された。
この系統のものは正倉院にある奈良時代の棚厨子が遺例として残り、後の時代の本棚に近く 天板と棚を二段渡しただけの簡素な造りのもので、この様な形をした棚は、『信貴山(しぎさん)縁起』『粉河寺(こかわでら)縁起』『石山寺縁起』『慕帰絵詞(ぼきえことば)』等の中世絵巻物で作中の台所に登場し、食物や食器などをのせている。
室内装飾を兼ねた調度品としては、身の回りの品々を整理し収納する為に、天武(てんむ)天皇から聖武(しょうむ)天皇に至る代々の天皇が伝えた愛用品だった。
仏具としての厨子
正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場しており、主に葬儀式においては[[創価学会]]が祭壇に据える。[[日蓮正宗]]の葬儀でも祭壇に置かれ、導師本尊が掲げられる。
近年、小型の仏壇として見直されている。
歴史的な作品としては[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の[[赤漆文欟木御厨子]]が有名。

正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場しており、主に葬儀式においては[[創価学会]]が祭壇に据える。[[日蓮正宗]]の葬儀でも祭壇に置かれ、導師本尊が掲げられる。
近年、小型の仏壇として見直されている。
歴史的な作品としては[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の[[赤漆文欟木御厨子]]が有名。

== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[玉虫厨子]] * [[赤漆文欟木御厨子]] * [[庄内厨子]]
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すし}} [[Category:仏具]]

'''厨子'''（ずし）は、収納具の一種であり、次のような用途で用いられるものである。'''龕'''（がん）ともいう。 *（もともとは）厨房で調理道具や食材等を納めるために使った収納具（「厨子」の「厨」は「厨房（ちゅうぼう、つまり台所、キッチン）」の「厨」と同じ字であり、料理・調理関連のことを指す字である）。 *（上から派生して）身の回りの品を納めるために使った収納具。室内装飾の役割も果たした調度品（家具）であった（つまり現代で言う「インテリア家具」でもあった。） *（上から派生したものだが）[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置するための収納具（これは仏龕（ぶつがん）ともいう）。[[仏具]]の一種という位置づけになる。[[仏壇]]も厨子の一種と分類することもできる。
== 概要 == ;現代のもの 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場しており、主に葬儀式においては[[創価学会]]が祭壇に据える。[[日蓮正宗]]の葬儀でも祭壇に置かれ、導師本尊が掲げられる。
近年、小型の仏壇として見直されている。
== 歴史 == もともと厨房(ちゅうぼう)（台所）で使用する道具類を入れる「いれもの」のことであった。 厨子は、もともと[[中国大陸]]にあったものであり<ref name="nihon_pedia">『日本大百科全書』【厨子】</ref>、それが[[奈良時代]]に日本に渡来した。
中国でも、日本でも（当記事の最初の定義文で挙げた）3つの用途の厨子があった。
厨房(台所)で使用する収納具が、厨房の外でも収納具として使われるようになり、転じて仏教用具を納める両扉の収納具としても用いられるようになったといわれている。
仏像、経巻、舎利、仏画などを納める仏具としの厨子は仏龕ともいう。（略して書く場合は）「豆子」とも書く。観音開きの扉をつけて漆や箔などを塗り装飾したものであり、ほとんどは木製で、屋形や筒形などがある。こうした形式は[[インド]]の[[石窟寺院]]の「龕(がん)」に基づくものともいわれるが、中国の『広弘明集(こうぐみょうしゅう)』第十六には「或は十尊五聖は共に一厨に処し、或は大士如来は倶(とも)に一櫃(ひつ)に蔵す」という一文があるので、すでに[[梁]]時代には中国で尊像類を厨子や櫃に安置することが行われていたとうかがえる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
;日本での歴史 食事道具の一種としての厨子の例としては奈良時代のものがあり、たとえば[[正倉院]]には「棚厨子」の実物が遺されており、これは天板と[[棚]]を2段渡しただけの簡単な形のもの（現代の素朴な本棚のようなもの）であった<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。またこうした形の厨子は『信貴山縁起(しぎさんえんぎ)』『粉河寺縁起(こかわでらえんぎ)』『石山寺縁起』『慕帰絵詞(ぼきえことば)』といった[[絵巻物]]の台所の場面に登場しており、食品や食器などが載せられている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
正倉院にはまた、伝来品の「柿厨子(かきずし）」「黒柿両面厨子」が納められており、それらは両開き扉付き、牙象の基台といった基本的な構造からなる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
身の回りの品々を整理し収納できる道具としての厨子は、天武天皇より聖武天皇に至る代々の天皇が、室内装飾を兼ねた調度品として愛好した<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。 正倉院には孝謙天皇が大仏に献じた「赤漆文欟木厨子(せきしつのぶんかんぼくのずし)」も納められており、これは[[ケヤキ]]の板に朱を塗りその上に透明な漆を塗っており（今日でいう「春慶塗」の技法）、両開き扉に鏁子(さし)をつけ、下部に牙象の基台を据え、内部には2段の棚があるものである。『東大寺献物帳』によると、この厨子には書物や、刀子(とうす)、尺、笏(しゃく)、[[尺八]]、犀角盃(さいかくはい)、双六(すごろく)などといった様々な品々が収納されている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />（これで天皇がどのような用途で使っていたかおおよそ推察できる。）
[[平安時代]]にはすでに一般[[庶民]]のあいだでも棚厨子が使用されていたということが様々な証拠で知られている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。たとえば絵巻物の『絵師草紙』の居間の場面に登場している厨子は３段のもので、上段には巻子(かんす)・巻紙・書状・[[刷毛]]、中段には黒塗りの箱・白木の箱、下段には木鉢・[[曲物]]・[[水瓶]]が置かれているのが描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。またたとえば『春日権現霊験記(かすがごんげんれいげんき)』の居間の場面に登場する厨子は2段で、上段に巻子・冊子・黒箱、下段には[[蒔絵]]の手箱が置かれているのが描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。庶民の厨子は、実生活で使用されるものを置くのに使われ、実用的な「白木造り」のものであったようである<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
歴史的な品としては特に[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の[[赤漆文欟木御厨子]]が有名である。
<gallery> [[ファイル:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|[[玉虫厨子]]（[[法隆寺]]・[[国宝]]）]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|栃木・光得寺 厨子入大日如来像（厨子・像ともに重要文化財）]] File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|伝・橘夫人念持仏厨子（法隆寺・国宝） File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|[[瑞巌寺]]（宮城県）本尊[[聖観音]]像を安置する厨子 ファイル:Miroku Zushi.jpg|木造弥勒菩薩半跏像を収める厨子（[[興福寺]]・[[重要文化財]]<ref>「厨子入木造弥勒菩薩半跏像」として</ref>） ファイル:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|[[赤漆文欟木御厨子]]（[[正倉院]]） ファイル:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|竹厨子（[[法隆寺献納宝物]]・国宝） </gallery>

== 関連項目 == * [[:en:Cupboard]]（カップボード） - 英語版記事。英語圏でも、もともとカップ類（食器類）を置くための収納具だったcupboard（カップボード）が、やがて台所以外の場所で様々なものを入れるための棚として使われるようになり、現在ではcupboardは箱状の棚全般、たとえば事務所などで使う棚も含めてcupboardという。

;日本での歴史 食事道具の一種としての厨子の例としては奈良時代のものがあり、たとえば[[正倉院]]には「棚厨子」の実物が遺されており、これは天板と[[棚]]を2段渡しただけの簡単な形のもの（現代の素朴な本棚のようなもの）であった<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。またこうした形の厨子は『信貴山縁起(しぎさんえんぎ)』『粉河寺縁起(こかわでらえんぎ)』『石山寺縁起』『慕帰絵詞(ぼきえことば)』といった[[絵巻物]]の台所の場面に登場しており、食品や食器などが載せられている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
正倉院にはまた、伝来品の「柿厨子(かきずし）」「黒柿両面厨子」が納められており、それらは両開き扉付き、牙象の基台といった基本的な構造からなる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
身の回りの品々を整理し収納できる道具としての厨子は、天武天皇より聖武天皇に至る代々の天皇が、室内装飾を兼ねた調度品として愛好した<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。 正倉院には孝謙天皇が大仏に献じた「赤漆文欟木厨子(せきしつのぶんかんぼくのずし)」も納められており、これは[[ケヤキ]]の板に朱を塗りその上に透明な漆を塗っており（今日でいう「春慶塗」の技法）、両開き扉に鏁子(さし)をつけ、下部に牙象の基台を据え、内部には2段の棚があるものである。『東大寺献物帳』によると、この厨子には書物や、刀子(とうす)、尺、笏(しゃく)、[[尺八]]、犀角盃(さいかくはい)、双六(すごろく)などといった様々な品々が収納されている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />（これで天皇がどのような用途で使っていたかおおよそ推察できる。）
[[平安時代]]にはすでに一般[[庶民]]のあいだでも棚厨子が使用されていたということが様々な証拠で知られている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。たとえば絵巻物の『絵師草紙』の居間の場面に登場している厨子は３段のもので、上段には巻子(かんす)・巻紙・書状・[[刷毛]]、中段には黒塗りの箱・白木の箱、下段には木鉢・[[曲物]]・[[水瓶]]が置かれているのが描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。またたとえば『春日権現霊験記(かすがごんげんれいげんき)』の居間の場面に登場する厨子は2段で、上段に巻子・冊子・黒箱、下段には[[蒔絵]]の手箱が置かれているのが描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。庶民の厨子は、実生活で使用されるものを置くのに使われ、実用的な「白木造り」のものであったようである<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
歴史的な品としては特に[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の[[赤漆文欟木御厨子]]が有名である。
<gallery> [[ファイル:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|[[玉虫厨子]]（[[法隆寺]]・[[国宝]]）]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|栃木・光得寺 厨子入大日如来像（厨子・像ともに重要文化財）]] File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|伝・橘夫人念持仏厨子（法隆寺・国宝） File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|[[瑞巌寺]]（宮城県）本尊[[聖観音]]像を安置する厨子 ファイル:Miroku Zushi.jpg|木造弥勒菩薩半跏像を収める厨子（[[興福寺]]・[[重要文化財]]<ref>「厨子入木造弥勒菩薩半跏像」として</ref>） ファイル:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|[[赤漆文欟木御厨子]]（[[正倉院]]） ファイル:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|竹厨子（[[法隆寺献納宝物]]・国宝） </gallery>
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[:en:Cupboard]]（カップボード） - 英語版記事。英語圏でも、もともとカップ類（食器類）を置くための収納具だったcupboard（カップボード）が、やがて台所以外の場所で様々なものを入れるための棚として使われるようになり、現在ではcupboardは箱状の棚全般、たとえば事務所などで使う棚も含めてcupboardという。
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すし}} [[Category:仏具]]

'''厨子'''（ずし）は、収納具の一種であり、次のような用途で用いられるものである。'''龕'''（がん）ともいう。 *厨房で調理道具や食材等を納めるために使った収納具（「厨」は「[[厨房]]」を指す）。 *（派生して）身の回りの品を納めるために使った収納具。室内装飾の役割も果たした。 *（派生して）[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置するための収納具。仏龕（ぶつがん）とも。[[仏具]]の一種と位置づけられる。[[仏壇]]も厨子の一種と分類することもできる。
== 概要 == ;現代のもの 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場している。
== 歴史 == もともと厨房で使用する道具類の収納具が、厨房の外でも使われるようになり、転じて仏具を納める両扉の収納具としても用いられるようになったといわれている。
、もともと[[中国大陸]]にあったもので<ref name="nihon_pedia">『日本大百科全書』【厨子】</ref>、日本には[[奈良時代]]に渡来した。
仏像、経巻、舎利、仏画などを納める仏具としての厨子は仏龕ともいう。略して「豆子」とも書く。観音開きの扉をつけて漆や箔などを塗り装飾したものであり、ほとんどは木製で、屋形や筒形などがある。こうした形式は[[インド]]の[[石窟寺院]]の「龕(がん)」に基づくものともいわれるが、中国の『[[広弘明集]]』第十六には「或は十尊五聖は共に一厨に処し、或は大士如来は倶(とも)に一櫃(ひつ)に蔵す」という一文があるので、すでに[[梁 (南朝)|梁]]時代には中国で尊像類を厨子や櫃に安置することが行われていたとうかがえる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
;日本での歴史 食事道具の一種としての厨子の例としては奈良時代のものがあり、たとえば[[正倉院]]には「棚厨子」の実物が遺されており、これは天板と[[棚]]を2段渡しただけの簡単な形のものであった<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。またこうした形の厨子は『[[信貴山縁起]]』『[[粉河寺縁起絵巻|粉河寺縁起]]』『[[石山寺縁起]]』『慕帰絵詞(ぼきえことば)』といった[[絵巻物]]の台所の場面に登場しており、食品や食器などが載せられている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
正倉院にはまた、伝来品の「柿厨子」「黒柿両面厨子」が納められており、それらは両開き扉付き、牙象の基台といった基本的な構造からなる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
身の回りの品々を整理し収納する厨子は、天武天皇より聖武天皇に至る代々の天皇が、室内装飾を兼ねた調度品として愛好した<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。 正倉院には孝謙天皇が大仏に献じた「[[赤漆文欟木御厨子|赤漆文欟木厨子]]」も納められており、これは[[ケヤキ]]の板に朱を塗りその上に透明な漆を塗っており、両開き扉に鏁子をつけ、下部に牙象の基台を据え、内部には2段の棚がある。『東大寺献物帳』によると、この厨子には書物や、刀子、尺、笏、[[尺八]]、犀角盃、双六などといった様々な品々が収納されている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
[[平安時代]]にはすでに一般[[庶民]]のあいだでも棚厨子が使用されていたことが様々な証拠で知られている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。たとえば『[[絵師草紙]]』の居間の場面に登場している厨子は３段のもので、上段には巻子・巻紙・書状・[[刷毛]]、中段には黒塗りの箱・白木の箱、下段には木鉢・[[曲物]]・[[水瓶]]が置かれているのが描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。また『[[春日権現験記|春日権現霊験記]]』の居間の場面に登場する厨子は2段で、上段に巻子・冊子・黒箱、下段には[[蒔絵]]の手箱が描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。庶民の厨子は、実生活で使用されるものを置くのに使われ、実用的な「白木造り」のものであったようである<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
歴史的な品としては特に[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の赤漆文欟木御厨子が有名である。

'''厨子'''（ずし）は、収納具の一種。おもに仏像や経典などを納めておく戸棚で、前面が両開きの戸になっている<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=室内学入門 |year=1995年 |publisher=建築資料研究社 |page=75 |author=渡辺優}}</ref>。次のような用途で用いられるものである。'''龕'''（がん）ともいう。 *もとは厨房用から始まった<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=室内学入門 |year=1995年 |publisher=建築資料研究社 |page=75 |author=渡辺優}}</ref>。厨房で調理道具や食材等を納めるために使った収納具（「厨」は「[[厨房]]」を指す）。 *（派生して）身の回りの品を納めるために使った収納具。室内装飾の役割も果たした。 *（派生して）[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置するための収納具。仏龕（ぶつがん）とも。[[仏具]]の一種と位置づけられる。[[仏壇]]も厨子の一種と分類することもできる。

、もともと[[中国大陸]]にあったもので<ref name="nihon_pedia">『日本大百科全書』【厨子】</ref>、日本には[[奈良時代]]に渡来した。

;日本での歴史 食事道具の一種としての厨子の例としては奈良時代のものがあり、たとえば[[正倉院]]には「棚厨子」の実物が遺されており、これは天板と[[棚]]を2段渡しただけの簡単な形のものであった<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。またこうした形の厨子は『[[信貴山縁起]]』『[[粉河寺縁起絵巻|粉河寺縁起]]』『[[石山寺縁起]]』『慕帰絵詞(ぼきえことば)』といった[[絵巻物]]の台所の場面に登場しており、食品や食器などが載せられている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
正倉院にはまた、伝来品の「柿厨子」「黒柿両面厨子」が納められており、それらは両開き扉付き、牙象の基台といった基本的な構造からなる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
身の回りの品々を整理し収納する厨子は、天武天皇より聖武天皇に至る代々の天皇が、室内装飾を兼ねた調度品として愛好した<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。 正倉院には孝謙天皇が大仏に献じた「[[赤漆文欟木御厨子|赤漆文欟木厨子]]」も納められており、これは[[ケヤキ]]の板に朱を塗りその上に透明な漆を塗っており、両開き扉に鏁子をつけ、下部に牙象の基台を据え、内部には2段の棚がある。『東大寺献物帳』によると、この厨子には書物や、刀子、尺、笏、[[尺八]]、犀角盃、双六などといった様々な品々が収納されている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
[[平安時代]]にはすでに一般[[庶民]]のあいだでも棚厨子が使用されていたことが様々な証拠で知られている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。たとえば『[[絵師草紙]]』の居間の場面に登場している厨子は３段のもので、上段には巻子・巻紙・書状・[[刷毛]]、中段には黒塗りの箱・白木の箱、下段には木鉢・[[曲物]]・[[水瓶]]が置かれているのが描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。また『[[春日権現験記|春日権現霊験記]]』の居間の場面に登場する厨子は2段で、上段に巻子・冊子・黒箱、下段には[[蒔絵]]の手箱が描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。庶民の厨子は、実生活で使用されるものを置くのに使われ、実用的な「白木造り」のものであったようである<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
歴史的な品としては特に[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の赤漆文欟木御厨子が有名である。
<gallery> [[ファイル:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|[[玉虫厨子]]（[[法隆寺]]・[[国宝]]）]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|栃木・光得寺 厨子入大日如来像（厨子・像ともに重要文化財）]] File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|伝・橘夫人念持仏厨子（法隆寺・国宝） File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|[[瑞巌寺]]（宮城県）本尊[[聖観音]]像を安置する厨子 ファイル:Miroku Zushi.jpg|木造弥勒菩薩半跏像を収める厨子（[[興福寺]]・[[重要文化財]]<ref>「厨子入木造弥勒菩薩半跏像」として</ref>） ファイル:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|[[赤漆文欟木御厨子]]（[[正倉院]]） ファイル:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|竹厨子（[[法隆寺献納宝物]]・国宝） </gallery>
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[:en:Cupboard]]（カップボード） - 英語版記事。英語圏でも、もともとカップ類（食器類）を置くための収納具だったcupboardが、やがて台所以外の場所で様々なものを入れるための棚として使われるようになり、現在では箱状の棚全般を指す。
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すし}} [[Category:仏具]]

'''厨子'''（ずし）は、収納具の一種。おもに仏像や経典などを納めておく戸棚で、前面が両開きの戸になっている<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=室内学入門 |year=1995 |publisher=建築資料研究社 |page=75 |author=渡辺優}}</ref>。次のような用途で用いられるものである。'''龕'''（がん）ともいう。 *もとは厨房用から始まった<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=室内学入門 |year=1995 |publisher=建築資料研究社 |page=75 |author=渡辺優}}</ref>。厨房で調理道具や食材等を納めるために使った収納具（「厨」は「[[厨房]]」を指す）。 *（派生して）身の回りの品を納めるために使った収納具。室内装飾の役割も果たした。 *（派生して）[[仏像]]・[[仏舎利]]・[[教典]]・[[位牌]]などを中に安置するための収納具。仏龕（ぶつがん）とも。[[仏具]]の一種と位置づけられる。[[仏壇]]も厨子の一種と分類することもできる。
== 概要 == ;現代のもの 正面に[[観音開き]]の扉が付く。[[漆]]塗りのものや、[[唐木]]、[[プラスチック]]製がある。 また手動で開くものに加え、最近では電動で扉が開閉するものが登場している。
== 歴史 == もともと厨房で使用する道具類の収納具が、厨房の外でも使われるようになり、転じて仏具を納める両扉の収納具としても用いられるようになったといわれている。
もともと[[中国大陸]]にあったもので<ref name="nihon_pedia">『日本大百科全書』【厨子】</ref>、日本には[[奈良時代]]に渡来した。
仏像、経巻、舎利、仏画などを納める仏具としての厨子は仏龕ともいう。略して「豆子」とも書く。観音開きの扉をつけて漆や箔などを塗り装飾したものであり、ほとんどは木製で、屋形や筒形などがある。こうした形式は[[インド]]の[[石窟寺院]]の「龕（がん）」に基づくものともいわれるが、中国の『[[広弘明集]]』第十六には「或は十尊五聖は共に一厨に処し、或は大士如来は倶（とも）に一櫃（ひつ）に蔵す」という一文があるので、すでに[[梁 (南朝)|梁]]時代には中国で尊像類を厨子や櫃に安置することが行われていたとうかがえる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
;日本での歴史 食事道具の一種としての厨子の例としては奈良時代のものがあり、たとえば[[正倉院]]には「棚厨子」の実物が遺されており、これは天板と[[棚]]を2段渡しただけの簡単な形のものであった<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。またこうした形の厨子は『[[信貴山縁起]]』『[[粉河寺縁起絵巻|粉河寺縁起]]』『[[石山寺縁起]]』『慕帰絵詞（ぼきえことば）』といった[[絵巻物]]の台所の場面に登場しており、食品や食器などが載せられている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
正倉院にはまた、伝来品の「柿厨子」「黒柿両面厨子」が納められており、それらは両開き扉付き、牙象の基台といった基本的な構造からなる<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
身の回りの品々を整理し収納する厨子は、天武天皇より聖武天皇に至る代々の天皇が、室内装飾を兼ねた調度品として愛好した<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。 正倉院には孝謙天皇が大仏に献じた「[[赤漆文欟木御厨子|赤漆文欟木厨子]]」も納められており、これは[[ケヤキ]]の板に朱を塗りその上に透明な漆を塗っており、両開き扉に鏁子をつけ、下部に牙象の基台を据え、内部には2段の棚がある。『東大寺献物帳』によると、この厨子には書物や、刀子、尺、笏、[[尺八]]、犀角盃、双六などといった様々な品々が収納されている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
[[平安時代]]にはすでに一般[[庶民]]のあいだでも棚厨子が使用されていたことが様々な証拠で知られている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。たとえば『[[絵師草紙]]』の居間の場面に登場している厨子は3段のもので、上段には巻子・巻紙・書状・[[刷毛]]、中段には黒塗りの箱・白木の箱、下段には木鉢・[[曲物]]・[[水瓶]]が置かれているのが描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。また『[[春日権現験記|春日権現霊験記]]』の居間の場面に登場する厨子は2段で、上段に巻子・冊子・黒箱、下段には[[蒔絵]]の手箱が描かれている<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。庶民の厨子は、実生活で使用されるものを置くのに使われ、実用的な「白木造り」のものであったようである<ref name="nihon_pedia" />。
歴史的な品としては特に[[法隆寺]]の[[玉虫厨子]]や[[正倉院]]の赤漆文欟木御厨子が有名である。

[[Category:厨子]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]]

厨子
[[Category:厨子]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[nl:Altaarkast]] [[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]]

'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Cupboard]] – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to **a shelf for cups and dishes**. Like zushi, it later evolved into **a general storage cabinet**.

Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

revision.text:
'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

[[Category:厨子]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[nl:Altaarkast]] [[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]]

'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

[[Category:厨子]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Zushi-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Zushi-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[en:Zushi]][[nl:Altaarkast]] [[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]]

'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.

'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

{{Buddhism-stub}} {{Buddhism2}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Zushi}} [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]]
[[Category:厨子]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Zushi-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Zushi-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Zushi}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Zushi]][[nl:Altaarkast]] [[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]]

'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

[[Category:厨子]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Zushi-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Zushi-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Zushi}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Zushi]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Zushi]][[nl:Altaarkast]] [[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]] [[Category:New]]

'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally **a kitchen storage unit** but later came to be used **outside the kitchen**, and eventually evolved into **a religious cabinet with double doors** for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.
As a **Buddhist altar cabinet**, it is also called **Butsugan (仏龕)**. The character **"豆子" (Zushi)** is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are **made of wood** and **decorated with lacquer or gold leaf**. They come in various forms, such as **roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases**.
This style is believed to be influenced by **rock-cut cave temples in India**, where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that **by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of **kitchen storage zushi** can be found from the **Nara period**. One such example is the **"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)** preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a **simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in **Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)** such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, **zushi are shown holding food items and dishes**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** also houses **"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)**, which **feature double-opening doors and ivory bases**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for **personal storage** were **favored by emperors** from **Emperor Tenmu** to **Emperor Shōmu**, serving as both **storage and interior decor**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|**[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** (Hōryū-ji, [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated **Maitreya Bodhisattva** (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|**Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

== Related Topics == * [[:en:Cupboard]] – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to **a shelf for cups and dishes**. Like zushi, it later evolved into **a general storage cabinet**.

revision.text:
'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing **Buddhist statues, sutras**, and other religious items. It is a **cabinet with double-opening doors** at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as **''gan'' (龕)**.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as **a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients** (since "厨" refers to "[[kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into **a storage unit for personal belongings**, also serving as **interior decoration**. * Eventually, it was used for **enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets**. It is also called a **Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")**, and is classified as a **Buddhist ritual item**. [[Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have **double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)**. They can be made from **lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic**. In addition to manually operated doors, **motorized automatic-opening zushi** are also available.

The concept of zushi originated in **mainland China**<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was **introduced to Japan during the Nara period**.

Additionally, **the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)** states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. The **Shōsōin repository** contains a **famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken** to the Great Buddha, called **"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)**. This **keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi** is **coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer**, with **double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base**. Inside, it had **two shelves**. According to the **"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")**, this zushi housed **books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the **Heian period**, **shelf zushi were widely used among commoners**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * **"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)** shows a **three-tiered zushi** containing **scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * **"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)** shows a **two-tiered zushi** with **scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the **decorative lacquered zushi** of aristocrats, **commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities**.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The **[[Tamamushi Shrine]]** at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The **Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi** at the **Shōsōin** repository.
