<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' (Hōryū-ji, [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Cupboard]] – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.

As a '''Buddhist altar cabinet''', it is also called '''Butsugan (仏龕)'''. The character '''"豆子" (Zushi)''' is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are '''made of wood''' and '''decorated with lacquer or gold leaf'''. They come in various forms, such as '''roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases'''.
This style is believed to be influenced by '''rock-cut cave temples in India''', where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, '''the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)''' states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that '''by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of '''kitchen storage zushi''' can be found from the '''Nara period'''. One such example is the '''"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)''' preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a '''simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in '''Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)''' such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, '''zushi are shown holding food items and dishes'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' also houses '''"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)''', which '''feature double-opening doors and ivory bases'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for '''personal storage''' were '''favored by emperors''' from '''Emperor Tenmu''' to '''Emperor Shōmu''', serving as both '''storage and interior decor'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' contains a '''famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken''' to the Great Buddha, called '''"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)'''. This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' (Hōryū-ji, [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Cupboard]] – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q19993032]] Wikidata name: zushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing '''Buddhist statues, sutras''', and other religious items. It is a '''cabinet with double-opening doors''' at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as '''''gan'' (龕)'''.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as '''a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients''' (since "厨" refers to "[[:en:Kitchen|Kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into '''a storage unit for personal belongings''', also serving as '''interior decoration'''. * Eventually, it was used for '''enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets'''. It is also called a '''Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")''', and is classified as a '''Buddhist ritual item'''. [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have '''double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)'''. They can be made from '''lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic'''. In addition to manually operated doors, '''motorized automatic-opening zushi''' are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally '''a kitchen storage unit''' but later came to be used '''outside the kitchen''', and eventually evolved into '''a religious cabinet with double doors''' for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in '''mainland China'''<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was '''introduced to Japan during the Nara period'''.
As a '''Buddhist altar cabinet''', it is also called '''Butsugan (仏龕)'''. The character '''"豆子" (Zushi)''' is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are '''made of wood''' and '''decorated with lacquer or gold leaf'''. They come in various forms, such as '''roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases'''.
This style is believed to be influenced by '''rock-cut cave temples in India''', where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, '''the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)''' states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that '''by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of '''kitchen storage zushi''' can be found from the '''Nara period'''. One such example is the '''"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)''' preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a '''simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in '''Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)''' such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, '''zushi are shown holding food items and dishes'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' also houses '''"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)''', which '''feature double-opening doors and ivory bases'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for '''personal storage''' were '''favored by emperors''' from '''Emperor Tenmu''' to '''Emperor Shōmu''', serving as both '''storage and interior decor'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' contains a '''famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken''' to the Great Buddha, called '''"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)'''. This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.

This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' (Hōryū-ji, [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Cupboard]] – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.

{{wikidata link|Q19993032}} [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q19993032]] Wikidata name: zushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing '''Buddhist statues, sutras''', and other religious items. It is a '''cabinet with double-opening doors''' at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as '''''gan'' (龕)'''.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as '''a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients''' (since "厨" refers to "[[:en:Kitchen|Kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into '''a storage unit for personal belongings''', also serving as '''interior decoration'''. * Eventually, it was used for '''enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets'''. It is also called a '''Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")''', and is classified as a '''Buddhist ritual item'''. [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have '''double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)'''. They can be made from '''lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic'''. In addition to manually operated doors, '''motorized automatic-opening zushi''' are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally '''a kitchen storage unit''' but later came to be used '''outside the kitchen''', and eventually evolved into '''a religious cabinet with double doors''' for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in '''mainland China'''<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was '''introduced to Japan during the Nara period'''.
As a '''Buddhist altar cabinet''', it is also called '''Butsugan (仏龕)'''. The character '''"豆子" (Zushi)''' is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are '''made of wood''' and '''decorated with lacquer or gold leaf'''. They come in various forms, such as '''roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases'''.
This style is believed to be influenced by '''rock-cut cave temples in India''', where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, '''the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)''' states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that '''by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of '''kitchen storage zushi''' can be found from the '''Nara period'''. One such example is the '''"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)''' preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a '''simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in '''Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)''' such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, '''zushi are shown holding food items and dishes'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' also houses '''"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)''', which '''feature double-opening doors and ivory bases'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for '''personal storage''' were '''favored by emperors''' from '''Emperor Tenmu''' to '''Emperor Shōmu''', serving as both '''storage and interior decor'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' contains a '''famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken''' to the Great Buddha, called '''"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)'''. This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' (Hōryū-ji, [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>

Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />

<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' (Hōryū-ji, [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Cupboard]] – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{translated page|ja|厨子|version=102803446|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[sn:Zushi]] [[en:Zushi]] [[nl:Altaarkast]] [[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]] [[sn:ushi]]
<!--Zushi--><!--Zushi--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Armoire d'autel]] [[gan:龕]] [[ko:감실]] [[zh:龕]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Armoire d'autel]] [[gan:龕]] [[ko:감실]] [[zh:龕]] {{wikidata link|Q19993032}} [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q19993032]] Wikidata name: zushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing '''Buddhist statues, sutras''', and other religious items. It is a '''cabinet with double-opening doors''' at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as '''''gan'' (龕)'''.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as '''a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients''' (since "厨" refers to "[[:en:Kitchen|Kitchen]]"). * Later, it evolved into '''a storage unit for personal belongings''', also serving as '''interior decoration'''. * Eventually, it was used for '''enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets'''. It is also called a '''Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")''', and is classified as a '''Buddhist ritual item'''. [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have '''double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)'''. They can be made from '''lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic'''. In addition to manually operated doors, '''motorized automatic-opening zushi''' are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally '''a kitchen storage unit''' but later came to be used '''outside the kitchen''', and eventually evolved into '''a religious cabinet with double doors''' for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in '''mainland China'''<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was '''introduced to Japan during the Nara period'''.
As a '''Buddhist altar cabinet''', it is also called '''Butsugan (仏龕)'''. The character '''"豆子" (Zushi)''' is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are '''made of wood''' and '''decorated with lacquer or gold leaf'''. They come in various forms, such as '''roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases'''.
This style is believed to be influenced by '''rock-cut cave temples in India''', where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, '''the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)''' states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that '''by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of '''kitchen storage zushi''' can be found from the '''Nara period'''. One such example is the '''"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)''' preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a '''simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in '''Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)''' such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, '''zushi are shown holding food items and dishes'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' also houses '''"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)''', which '''feature double-opening doors and ivory bases'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for '''personal storage''' were '''favored by emperors''' from '''Emperor Tenmu''' to '''Emperor Shōmu''', serving as both '''storage and interior decor'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' contains a '''famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken''' to the Great Buddha, called '''"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)'''. This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''[[:en:Tamamushi Shrine|Tamamushi Shrine]]''' (Hōryū-ji, [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]])]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Cupboard]] – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{translated page|ja|厨子|version=102803446|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' (Hōryū-ji, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}})]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cupboard|en|Cupboard|WD=Q5662058|lt=Cupboard}} – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{translated page|ja|厨子|version=102803446|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[sn:Zushi]] [[en:Zushi]] [[nl:Altaarkast]] [[fr:Armoire d%27autel]] [[ko:%EA%B0%90%EC%8B%A4]] [[zh:%E9%BE%95]] [[ja:厨子]] [[sn:ushi]]
<!--Zushi--><!--Zushi--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Armoire d'autel]] [[gan:龕]] [[ko:감실]] [[zh:龕]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Armoire d'autel]] [[gan:龕]] [[ko:감실]] [[zh:龕]] {{wikidata link|Q19993032}} [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q19993032]] Wikidata name: zushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing '''Buddhist statues, sutras''', and other religious items. It is a '''cabinet with double-opening doors''' at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as '''''gan'' (龕)'''.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as '''a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients''' (since "厨" refers to "{{ill|Kitchen|en|Kitchen|WD=Q43164|lt=Kitchen}}"). * Later, it evolved into '''a storage unit for personal belongings''', also serving as '''interior decoration'''. * Eventually, it was used for '''enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets'''. It is also called a '''Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")''', and is classified as a '''Buddhist ritual item'''. {{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|WD=Q327521|lt=Butsudan}} (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have '''double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)'''. They can be made from '''lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic'''. In addition to manually operated doors, '''motorized automatic-opening zushi''' are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally '''a kitchen storage unit''' but later came to be used '''outside the kitchen''', and eventually evolved into '''a religious cabinet with double doors''' for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in '''mainland China'''<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was '''introduced to Japan during the Nara period'''.
As a '''Buddhist altar cabinet''', it is also called '''Butsugan (仏龕)'''. The character '''"豆子" (Zushi)''' is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are '''made of wood''' and '''decorated with lacquer or gold leaf'''. They come in various forms, such as '''roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases'''.
This style is believed to be influenced by '''rock-cut cave temples in India''', where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, '''the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)''' states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that '''by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of '''kitchen storage zushi''' can be found from the '''Nara period'''. One such example is the '''"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)''' preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a '''simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in '''Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)''' such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, '''zushi are shown holding food items and dishes'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' also houses '''"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)''', which '''feature double-opening doors and ivory bases'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for '''personal storage''' were '''favored by emperors''' from '''Emperor Tenmu''' to '''Emperor Shōmu''', serving as both '''storage and interior decor'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' contains a '''famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken''' to the Great Buddha, called '''"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)'''. This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' (Hōryū-ji, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}})]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cupboard|en|Cupboard|WD=Q5662058|lt=Cupboard}} – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{translated page|ja|厨子|version=102803446|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' (Hōryū-ji, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}})]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cupboard|en|Cupboard|WD=Q5662058|lt=Cupboard}} – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]]
'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing '''Buddhist statues, sutras''', and other religious items. It is a '''cabinet with double-opening doors''' at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as '''''gan'' (龕)'''.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as '''a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients''' (since "厨" refers to "{{ill|Kitchen|en|Kitchen|WD=Q43164|lt=Kitchen|1=Kitchen}}"). * Later, it evolved into '''a storage unit for personal belongings''', also serving as '''interior decoration'''. * Eventually, it was used for '''enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets'''. It is also called a '''Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")''', and is classified as a '''Buddhist ritual item'''. {{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|WD=Q327521|lt=Butsudan|1=Butsudan}} (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have '''double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)'''. They can be made from '''lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic'''. In addition to manually operated doors, '''motorized automatic-opening zushi''' are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally '''a kitchen storage unit''' but later came to be used '''outside the kitchen''', and eventually evolved into '''a religious cabinet with double doors''' for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in '''mainland China'''<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was '''introduced to Japan during the Nara period'''.
As a '''Buddhist altar cabinet''', it is also called '''Butsugan (仏龕)'''. The character '''"豆子" (Zushi)''' is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are '''made of wood''' and '''decorated with lacquer or gold leaf'''. They come in various forms, such as '''roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases'''.
This style is believed to be influenced by '''rock-cut cave temples in India''', where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, '''the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)''' states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that '''by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of '''kitchen storage zushi''' can be found from the '''Nara period'''. One such example is the '''"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)''' preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a '''simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in '''Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)''' such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, '''zushi are shown holding food items and dishes'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' also houses '''"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)''', which '''feature double-opening doors and ivory bases'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for '''personal storage''' were '''favored by emperors''' from '''Emperor Tenmu''' to '''Emperor Shōmu''', serving as both '''storage and interior decor'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' contains a '''famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken''' to the Great Buddha, called '''"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)'''. This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' (Hōryū-ji, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}})]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cupboard|en|Cupboard|WD=Q5662058|lt=Cupboard|1=Cupboard}} – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{translated page|ja|厨子|version=102803446|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' (Hōryū-ji, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}})]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cupboard|en|Cupboard|WD=Q5662058|lt=Cupboard|1=Cupboard}} – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Armoire d'autel]][[gan:龕]][[ja:厨子]][[ko:감실]][[nl:Altaarkast]][[zh:龕]]
'''Zushi''' (厨子) is a type of storage furniture, primarily used for storing '''Buddhist statues, sutras''', and other religious items. It is a '''cabinet with double-opening doors''' at the front.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref> It is also known as '''''gan'' (龕)'''.
Zushi can serve the following purposes: * Originally, it was used in kitchens<ref>{{Cite book|title=Introduction to Interior Studies |year=1995 |publisher=Architecture Materials Research Institute |page=75 |author=Yutaka Watanabe}}</ref>, as '''a storage unit for cooking tools and ingredients''' (since "厨" refers to "{{ill|Kitchen|en|Kitchen|WD=Q43164|lt=Kitchen|1=Kitchen}}"). * Later, it evolved into '''a storage unit for personal belongings''', also serving as '''interior decoration'''. * Eventually, it was used for '''enshrining Buddhist statues, relics, scriptures, and memorial tablets'''. It is also called a '''Butsugan (仏龕, "Buddhist niche")''', and is classified as a '''Buddhist ritual item'''. {{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|WD=Q327521|lt=Butsudan|1=Butsudan}} (Buddhist altars) can also be considered a type of zushi.
== Overview == ; Modern zushi Modern zushi typically have '''double-opening doors (hinged like a cabinet)'''. They can be made from '''lacquered wood, rosewood, or plastic'''. In addition to manually operated doors, '''motorized automatic-opening zushi''' are also available.
== History == The zushi was originally '''a kitchen storage unit''' but later came to be used '''outside the kitchen''', and eventually evolved into '''a religious cabinet with double doors''' for housing Buddhist artifacts.
The concept of zushi originated in '''mainland China'''<ref name="nihon_pedia">Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (日本大百科全書), "Zushi (厨子)"</ref>, and was '''introduced to Japan during the Nara period'''.
As a '''Buddhist altar cabinet''', it is also called '''Butsugan (仏龕)'''. The character '''"豆子" (Zushi)''' is sometimes used as an abbreviation. Traditional zushi are '''made of wood''' and '''decorated with lacquer or gold leaf'''. They come in various forms, such as '''roofed cabinets or cylindrical cases'''.
This style is believed to be influenced by '''rock-cut cave temples in India''', where niches ("gan" 龕) were carved into stone to enshrine images. Additionally, '''the Chinese text "Guang Hongming Ji" (広弘明集, Volume 16)''' states, "Some enshrined ten revered Buddhas together in a single zushi, while others placed great Bodhisattvas and Tathagatas together in one chest." This indicates that '''by the Liang dynasty (502–557 AD), placing Buddhist images in zushi or chests was already common in China'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
; History in Japan Examples of '''kitchen storage zushi''' can be found from the '''Nara period'''. One such example is the '''"Tana-zushi" (shelf zushi)''' preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] repository. This was a '''simple two-tier wooden shelf with a top board'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
This type of kitchen storage is also depicted in '''Japanese illustrated scrolls (emaki)''' such as: * ''[[Shigisan Engi]]'' * ''[[Kokawadera Engi Emaki]]'' * ''[[Ishiyamadera Engi]]'' * ''Boki Ekotoba (慕帰絵詞)''
In these scrolls, '''zushi are shown holding food items and dishes'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' also houses '''"Kaki Zushi" (柿厨子) and "Kurokaki Ryōmen Zushi" (黒柿両面厨子)''', which '''feature double-opening doors and ivory bases'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Zushi for '''personal storage''' were '''favored by emperors''' from '''Emperor Tenmu''' to '''Emperor Shōmu''', serving as both '''storage and interior decor'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
The '''Shōsōin repository''' contains a '''famous zushi dedicated by Empress Kōken''' to the Great Buddha, called '''"Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi" (赤漆文欟木厨子)'''. This '''keyaki wood (zelkova) zushi''' is '''coated in red lacquer and clear lacquer''', with '''double doors, a locking mechanism, and an ivory base'''. Inside, it had '''two shelves'''. According to the '''"Tōdaiji Kenmotsu Chō" (東大寺献物帳, "Tōdaiji Offering Records")''', this zushi housed '''books, knives, rulers, scepters, shakuhachi flutes, rhinoceros horn cups, and even board games'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
By the '''Heian period''', '''shelf zushi were widely used among commoners'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> Illustrated evidence includes: * '''"Eseshi Sōshi" (絵師草紙)''' shows a '''three-tiered zushi''' containing '''scrolls, brushes, boxes, bowls, and water jars'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" /> * '''"Kasuga Gongen Reigenki" (春日権現霊験記)''' shows a '''two-tiered zushi''' with '''scrolls, black boxes, and maki-e lacquered chests'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' (Hōryū-ji, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}})]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cupboard|en|Cupboard|WD=Q5662058|lt=Cupboard|1=Cupboard}} – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{translated page|ja|厨子|version=102803446|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Unlike the '''decorative lacquered zushi''' of aristocrats, '''commoners' zushi were made of plain wood and used for daily necessities'''.<ref name="nihon_pedia" />
Historically significant zushi include: * The '''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' at [[Hōryū-ji]] (a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}) * The '''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' at the '''Shōsōin''' repository.
<gallery> [[File:Tamamushi Shrine.JPG|thumb|right|180px|'''{{ill|Tamamushi Shrine|en|Tamamushi Shrine|WD=Q7680611|lt=Tamamushi Shrine|1=Tamamushi Shrine}}''' (Hōryū-ji, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}})]] [[File:Dainichi Nyorai.jpg|thumb|180px|Tochigi – Kōtoku-ji, Zushi with Dainichi Nyorai (both Important Cultural Properties)]] [[File:Tachibana Shrine (Asahi Shimbunsha 1942).jpg|thumb|180px|Alleged Zushi for Empress Tachibana’s personal Buddhist image (Hōryū-ji, National Treasure)]] [[File:Principal image Buddha of Zuigan-ji Temple.JPG|thumb|180px|Zushi enshrining [[Shō Kannon]] at [[Zuigan-ji]], Miyagi Prefecture]] [[File:Miroku Zushi.jpg|thumb|180px|Zushi housing a seated '''Maitreya Bodhisattva''' (Kōfuku-ji, [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])]] [[File:RED LACQUERED CABINET Shosoin N2.JPG|thumb|180px|'''Aka-urushi Monkanboku Zushi''' (Shōsōin)]] [[File:Bamboo Cabinet Horuji.jpg|thumb|180px|Bamboo Zushi (Hōryū-ji Treasures, National Treasure)]] </gallery>
== References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cupboard|en|Cupboard|WD=Q5662058|lt=Cupboard|1=Cupboard}} – The English word "cupboard" originally referred to '''a shelf for cups and dishes'''. Like zushi, it later evolved into '''a general storage cabinet'''.
